_meta.description: Simulation state lookup table for ScaleCraft URL Shortener lesson. Numbers are illustrative — directionally correct, not production measurements.

dimensions.caching: boolean — Redis cache in front of database
dimensions.traffic_level: low (<200 RPS) | med (<500 RPS) | high (<900 RPS) | spike (900+ RPS)

fields.rps_capacity: Approximate max RPS before system degrades further
fields.cache_hit_rate_pct: Percentage of reads served from cache (0 if caching=false)
fields.toggle_explanation: Plain-English explanation of why these numbers are what they are

states.toggle_explanation: At low traffic, a single database handles the load fine. 18ms p50 is the round-trip cost of one PostgreSQL query with a sequential scan on an unindexed column. No errors — the connection pool isn't saturated yet.

At medium traffic, PostgreSQL's default 100-connection limit starts binding. Some requests queue for a connection, adding 30–100ms. The 0.5% error rate is connection pool exhaustion — PostgreSQL is rejecting new connections when all 100 are busy.

High traffic saturates the database connection pool. Most requests queue 100–400ms waiting for a free connection. 4.2% of requests time out before getting a connection at all. The database CPU is at ~85% from query processing.

System is near failure. 18.5% error rate means roughly 1 in 5 requests fails. The database is at 100% CPU. Connection queue depth is 500+ — requests wait 800ms+ just for a connection slot, then the query itself takes another 400ms. This is the bottleneck you saw in 2008-era web startups.

Cache hit rate ~87% → 87 of 100 requests return in <1ms from Redis. The 13 cache misses still hit PostgreSQL at ~18ms. Blended p50 is ~3ms. System capacity jumps from 180 RPS to 2,800 RPS because Redis handles 100,000+ reads/sec.
Cache hit rate climbs slightly as the working set warms up. Database only sees ~11% of reads — about 55 RPS instead of 450 RPS. Connection pool pressure is negligible. Zero errors.

Cache absorbs 90% of reads. Database sees ~90 RPS — well within capacity. The 0.1% error rate is occasional cache misses during hot access patterns where multiple requests race to populate the same missing key (cache stampede, rare). p99 of 28ms is the tail for cache misses that hit a momentarily busy database.

At spike traffic, cache hit rate peaks at 91% as the working set is fully warm. Database still sees ~9% of 1,200 RPS = ~108 RPS — manageable. The 0.8% error rate comes from the write path: short link creation (writes go to DB, bypass cache) creates brief connection contention. Cache is carrying the read side perfectly.

Read replica routes 90% of reads to a separate DB instance. The primary only handles writes (~10% of traffic). At low traffic, latency is similar to no-replica — the bottleneck isn't DB capacity yet, it's the query round-trip time. RPS capacity roughly doubles.

Replica handles ~450 read RPS, primary handles ~50 write RPS. Connection pool pressure is halved vs. no-replica. Zero errors because the replica has its own independent 100-connection pool — reads no longer compete with writes for connections.

Replica is now saturating. 800 RPS × 90% = 720 read RPS hitting one replica instance. Connection pool on the replica is queueing. Errors are replica connection timeouts. Primary is fine (only 80 write RPS). The read replica helped significantly — halved error rate vs. no-replica — but didn't eliminate the problem.

Replica is fully saturated. You've doubled read capacity vs. no-replica (from ~500 RPS to ~950 RPS), but at spike traffic you've just hit the replica's wall instead of the primary's. 9.5% errors — better than 18.5% without replica, but still failing for 1 in 10 users. You need a cache to get off this treadmill.

PgBouncer pools connections at the application layer — your 20 API server threads share a pool of 20 persistent DB connections instead of opening 20 new connections per request. Connection setup overhead (~5ms) is eliminated. p50 drops slightly. At low traffic, the effect is minimal.

Connection pooling eliminates the 0.5% error rate you'd see without it at this traffic level. PgBouncer queues requests that exceed the pool size instead of failing them — requests wait a bit longer but don't error. p99 climbs slightly from queueing, but error rate stays at zero.

Connection pooling reduced errors from 4.2% to 0.5% — the pool is absorbing connection spikes that used to cause hard failures. But p99 is still 310ms: the database itself is the bottleneck, not connection management. Pooling helped throughput, not query speed. The 0.5% errors are PgBouncer queue timeouts when wait time exceeds 10s.

At spike traffic, connection pooling is heroic — it halved your error rate from 18.5% to 8%. But you're still failing for 1 in 12 users because the database is the bottleneck, not the connection layer. Every request still waits for PostgreSQL to execute a query on a table with millions of rows. You need a cache.

All three fixes active. Cache handles 88% of reads in <1ms. Read replica handles the 12% of reads that miss cache. PgBouncer keeps connections efficient. Primary database only sees writes. At low traffic, the system is dramatically over-provisioned — everything is fast and idle.

All three fixes synergize. Cache hit rate climbs as cache warms. Replica handles the 9% of cache misses. Connection pool keeps the replica's connection count stable. The primary sees ~50 write RPS. Every component is running well within capacity.

System handles high traffic easily. Cache at 92% hit rate means replica sees only ~72 RPS (8% of 900 RPS). PgBouncer prevents connection storms on the replica. Primary handles ~90 write RPS without contention. This is the architecture that handles millions of daily active users.

System fixed. 0% error rate at spike traffic. Cache absorbs 93% of 1,200 RPS = 1,116 requests/sec from Redis at <1ms. Replica handles the remaining 84 read RPS. Connection pool keeps both database tiers stable. This is what 'horizontally scalable' looks like in practice.

At 99% reads, your write path is nearly idle. The database sees almost exclusively SELECT queries — no write lock contention. Latency is slightly better than 90% reads because there's no row locking from concurrent writes. Capacity is similar.

Still near failure, but slightly better than 90% reads. The 1% write rate means writes aren't creating lock contention on the database. But the read volume alone is enough to saturate connections. 17% error rate vs. 18.5% at 90% read ratio — the difference is only 1.5 percentage points. A database without a cache fails under read load regardless of read ratio.

Here's where the read ratio matters. At 99% reads with caching on, cache hit rate climbs to 96% — higher than 91% at 90% reads. Why? Fewer unique writes means the cached entries stay valid longer and the working set is smaller. The database sees only 4% of spike traffic (~48 RPS) instead of 9% (~108 RPS). p99 drops from 52ms to 38ms. A URL shortener with viral content (same URLs shared repeatedly) sees exactly this pattern.

Best case scenario. 97% cache hit rate — 3 of 100 requests reach the database. The replica handles those 3% with room to spare. Connection pooling keeps the replica stable. The system's theoretical limit is now Redis's throughput (~100,000 reads/sec on a single instance), not the database. This is what Twitter's t.co link shortener looks like at scale: nearly every redirect is a cache hit.
